items.title: Effectiveness of emergency contraception
items.contentHtml:
Effectiveness of emergency contraception

  • The effectiveness of emergency contraception varies depending on the method and timing of administration compared to unprotected sexual intercourse.
  • ulipristal acetate (30 mg, single dose) is more effective than levonorgestrel (1.5 mg, single dose) for up to 120 hours, with a pregnancy rate of 1.4%. vs. 2.6%.
  • The copper intrauterine device is the most effective method (>99% risk reduction) if inserted within 5 days after sexual intercourse.
  • Emergency contraception with levonorgestrel progressively loses effectiveness after the first 24 hours; It is not recommended beyond 72-96 hours.

items.title: WHO eligibility criteria
items.contentHtml:
WHO eligibility criteria

  • The World Health Organization classifies eligibility into 4 categories: Category 1 (no restriction), Category 2 (benefits outweigh risks), Category 3 (risks outweigh benefits, use not recommended unless otherwise impossible) and Category 4 (unacceptable risk, contraindicated).
  • Example: uncontrolled arterial hypertension (>160/100 mmHg) is Category 4 for combined oral contraceptives.
  • Breastfeeding between 6 weeks and 6 months postpartum: progestin-only contraceptives are Category 2; combined are Category 3.
  • History of breast cancer: all hormonal contraceptives are Category 4.

items.foco: Mechanism of action of the copper IUD
items.title: Mechanism of action of the copper IUD
items.contentHtml:
Mechanism of action of the copper intrauterine device

  • The copper intrauterine device releases copper ions that generate a sterile inflammatory reaction in the endometrium, altering sperm motility and capacitation.
  • The endometrial environment is toxic for sperm, inhibiting fertilization; It also prevents implantation if fertilization occurs.
  • Does not interfere with ovulation; Its main effect is spermicidal and preimplantation.
  • Insertion within 5 days after intercourse is effective as emergency contraception.

items.title: Contraindications of combined oral contraceptives
items.contentHtml:
Contraindications of combined oral contraceptives

  • Absolute contraindications (World Health Organization Category 4) include: smoking over 35 years of age (>15 cigarettes/day), uncontrolled arterial hypertension (>160/100 mmHg), migraine with aura, Previous or current venous thromboembolism, and Breast cancer.
  • Active liver disease (acute hepatitis, decompensated cirrhosis) is an absolute contraindication.
  • Breastfeeding <6 weeks postpartum is Category 4 due to the passage of estrogen to the infant and the risk of decreased production.
  • Major surgery with prolonged immobilization: stop combined oral contraceptives 4 weeks before.

items.title: Use of contraception during breastfeeding
items.contentHtml:
Use of contraception during breastfeeding

  • In exclusive breastfeeding (<6 months, amenorrhea) the breastfeeding and amenorrhea method (LAM) is >98% effective if: breastfeeding on demand, without supplements and amenorrhea are met.
  • Progestin-only contraceptives (progestin-only pill, implant, acetate injectable of medroxyprogesterone) are safe from 6 weeks postpartum (World Health Organization Category 2).
  • Combined oral contraceptives are contraindicated in the first 6 weeks (Category 4); between 6 weeks and 6 months are Category 3.
  • The copper intrauterine device can be inserted as early as 48 hours postpartum if there is no infection.

items.title: Adverse effects of subdermal implants
items.contentHtml:
Adverse effects of subdermal implants

  • The most common adverse effect is alteration of the menstrual pattern: irregular bleeding, spotting or amenorrhea occurs in up to 60% of users during the first year.
  • Other effects: headache, acne, weight gain (modest, average 1-2 kg), mastalgia and mood changes.
  • Local complications: infection at the insertion site (<1%), spontaneous expulsion (rare) and local pain.
  • The etonogestrel implant (68 mg) lasts 3 years; levonorgestrel (2 sticks) lasts 5 years.

items.foco: Indications for emergency contraception with levonorgestrel
items.title: Indications for emergency contraception with levonorgestrel
items.contentHtml:
Indications for emergency contraception with levonorgestrel

  • Levonorgestrel (1.5 mg single dose) is indicated for emergency contraception up to 72 hours after unprotected sexual intercourse or failure of the contraceptive method.
  • It can be used in any menstrual cycle, without the need for a prior pregnancy test if menstruation was normal.
  • Contraindications: hypersensitivity to drug; There are no absolute contraindications based on World Health Organization eligibility criteria.
  • Less effective than ulipristal acetate (30 mg) or copper intrauterine device, especially if body mass index is >30 kg/m².

items.foco: Management of amenorrhea in contraceptive users
items.title: Management of amenorrhea in contraceptive users
items.contentHtml:
Management of amenorrhea in contraceptive users

  • amenorrhea is common with progestin-only contraceptives (implant, hormonal intrauterine device, injectable medroxyprogesterone acetate) and does not require treatment if the user is asymptomatic and has a negative pregnancy test.
  • In users of combined oral contraceptives, Amenorrhea may be due to endometrial thinning; pregnancy should be ruled out and adherence evaluated.
  • If amenorrhea is prolonged (>6 months) and bothersome, a method with estrogen or a progestogen cycle can be changed.
  • In the hormonal intrauterine device (levonorgestrel), amenorrhea is expected and reversible upon removal.

items.title: Drug interactions with hormonal contraceptives
items.contentHtml:
Drug interactions with hormonal contraceptives

  • Hepatic enzyme inducers (CYP3A4) reduce the effectiveness of hormonal contraceptives: rifampicin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, topiramate and herb. San Juan.
  • Rifampin reduces the concentration of ethinyl estradiol up to 40%; Additional barrier method is recommended during treatment and 28 days after.
  • Non-inducing antibiotics (such as amoxicillin, doxycycline) have no clinically significant interaction with hormonal contraceptives.
  • Combined oral contraceptives can increase the effect of benzodiazepines (diazepam) and decrease that of lamotrigine, reducing its seizure control.

items.foco: Hormonal IUD vs copper IUD differences
items.title: Hormonal IUD vs copper IUD differences
items.contentHtml:
Hormonal intrauterine device vs copper intrauterine device differences

  • The hormonal intrauterine device (levonorgestrel) releases local progestogen, thickening the cervical mucus and inhibiting endometrial proliferation; may suppress ovulation in some cycles.
  • The copper intrauterine device does not contain hormones; It acts due to sperm toxicity and inflammatory reaction.
  • The hormonal intrauterine device reduces menstrual bleeding (amenorrhea by 20-40% at 1 year), while the copper one increases bleeding and cramps.
  • Duration: copper intrauterine device 10-12 years; hormonal intrauterine device (levonorgestrel 52 mg) 5-7 years depending on model.

items.title: Criteria for starting postpartum contraception
items.contentHtml:
Criteria for starting postpartum contraception

  • Non-hormonal contraceptives (copper intrauterine device, barrier methods) can be started immediately after delivery, regardless of breastfeeding.
  • Progestin-only contraceptives (implant, medroxyprogesterone acetate injectable, progestin-only pill) are started 6 weeks postpartum if there is lactation; without breastfeeding, they can be started immediately (World Health Organization Category 1).
  • Combined oral contraceptives: start at 6 weeks if not breastfeeding; If breastfeeding, wait up to 6 months or until weaning.
  • The insertion of the hormonal intrauterine device is recommended at 4-6 weeks postpartum to reduce the risk of expulsion.

items.title: Effectiveness of barrier methods
items.contentHtml:
Effectiveness of barrier methods

  • The male condom has a typical effectiveness of 82% (perfect use 98%) in preventing pregnancy; It also protects against sexually transmitted infections.
  • The female condom has a typical efficacy of 79% (perfect use 95%).
  • The diaphragm with spermicide has a typical efficacy of 88% (perfect use 94%); requires professional adjustment and placement prior to intercourse.
  • The vaginal sponge (with nonoxynol-9) has typical efficacy of 76-88% depending on parity; less effectiveness in women who have had vaginal births.
